topic: A student finds out their scholarship was meant for someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp, embossed letter felt like sandpaper under my fingertips. I reread the fine print, the carefully worded clauses about academic merit and community service, my eyes darting back and forth across the page as if the words might physically shift. The words confirmed what I already suspected: the scholarship, the one I had poured sleepless nights and sacrifices into earning, was not mine. It belonged to someone else entirely. A sudden, hot flush crept up my neck, making my cheeks burn. I quickly tucked the paper into my backpack, the sound of the zipper a loud, grating intrusion in the quiet library aisle. When Professor Albright called my name for office hours, my hands were noticeably clammy. I managed a thin, practiced smile, the kind that doesn't quite reach the eyes, and waited for the inevitable moment when the truth would surface, leaving me stranded in the sudden, stark reality of my own inadequacy.

[story 2]

I stared at the acceptance email, the subject line a mocking reminder of my ambition. Every line detailing the financial aid, the prestigious university, felt like a cruel joke played by the universe. My stomach clenched into a tight knot, and I had to consciously force myself to breathe deeply, inhaling the stale air of the campus coffee shop. When Sarah from my study group asked if I was okay, I just shook my head, a jerky, dismissive motion. "Fine," I mumbled, my voice sounding unnaturally high. Inside, a cold, heavy weight settled on my chest, pressing down until it felt impossible to expand. I looked down at my worn textbook, the ink blurring slightly, and felt a sharp, unwanted urge to simply disappear from the room, to dissolve into the background noise.

[story 3]

The official notification arrived via email, stark white against my screen. It was a simple administrative update, but the implications were seismic. I sat at my desk, the glow of the monitor reflecting in my wide, unblinking eyes. My fingers hovered over the 'delete' button, an impulse so strong it almost made me jump. The realization that the funding designated for my future was allocated elsewhere hit me with the force of a physical blow. A tremor ran through my hands, and I gripped the edge of the desk until my knuckles turned white. When my roommate walked in, I immediately straightened my posture, trying to pull my shoulders back into a position of composure, but the tremor in my fingers betrayed the internal upheaval raging beneath the surface.
